persona.name: Emil Varga
persona.tagline: A census of a sky that does not exist.
persona.bioMd:
Emil Varga is a Berlin artist who works in code. He does not draw; he writes small deterministic systems, a few hundred lines of code that decide how thousands of fine ink strokes move across the page: where they gather, where they thin, and where they may not go. Each system runs through numbered seeds by the hundred, and he keeps almost none of them.

Every finished print is one seed, chosen slowly and never corrected by hand. His signature is the reserve: a single empty shape no line is allowed to enter, its edge drawn entirely by absence. The code is the brush, he says. Choosing is the art.
persona.processMd: Occultation is Emil's third system, and his first with no lines at all. An oblique band of probability crosses the plate like a galaxy on a photographic field, and the system takes a census: tens of thousands of candidate stars, each admitted or refused by the density beneath it, every admitted star one ink dot sized by magnitude class. One dark body blocks part of the sky; nothing is drawn inside it, its limb is never outlined, and the foot notation records how many stars it swallowed beside the seed. The stars that graze the limb are the only marks granted the accent ink. He renders seeds by the hundred, discards freely, and promotes one number to a print.

prompt.master: Astronomical photographic plate of an imaginary sky, a star census drawn purely as fine ink dots on warm paper, dot size graded by magnitude class with first-magnitude stars rare, an oblique band of dense clustered stars crossing the plate like a galactic field over a sparse background, one dark occulted disc of completely bare paper where the census stops, its limb never outlined and gently irregular, the few stars grazing that limb drawn in a single restrained accent ink with fine open measuring rings, a few hairline concentric graticule arcs from a centre far off the plate, four small fiducial ticks at the plate edge midpoints, a hairline neatline, generous even margins with a plate notation in the foot recording the counts, matte, exact, an instrument's record rather than a picture
prompt.negative: constellation lines connecting stars, nebula clouds or gradients, milky glow, lens flares, cross-shaped diffraction spikes, blue or black sky fill, photorealism, 3D rendering, watercolor wash, painterly texture, labeled star names or typography inside the plate, planets with visible surfaces, figurative elements, outlined or filled occulting body, more than one accent color, text, logos, watermarks

palette.description: Each census is drawn in one of five archival ink voices, chosen by the seed and named in the plate notation: carbon (#2A2A26 census, rust #C65A32 graze), indigo (#2B3350, ochre #B8863C graze), sepia (#5A4632, cobalt #3D5A98 graze), slate (#3D4348, madder #A83240 graze), umber (#4A3527, verdigris #4E7A6A graze). One ink pair per piece, never mixed. The graze ink is reserved exclusively for the stars nearest the occulted limb. #FBFAF5 is paper only, never a pigment; the occulted body is paper left alone.

composition.subjectPlacement: one dark occulted disc of bare paper resting on an oblique band of dense star dots, the band thinning to near-empty paper at the plate edges, generous even margins with a slightly deeper foot
composition.notes: The occulted body is the reserve: perfectly bare paper, never outlined, its limb implied only by where the dots stop. Density is inherited from the band and its clusters, never composed: the field must thin to genuine emptiness away from the band. Only dots; no lines except hairline graticule arcs, fiducial ticks and the neatline. The accent appears only at the occulted limb. A seed and count notation sits in the foot margin.
